testCases.name: TC_DOM_001
testCases.input: What happens after a dental implant is inserted into the jawbone?
testCases.actualOutput: After a dental implant is inserted into the jawbone, there is a healing period of three to six months, known as osseointegration. Following this period, an abutment and a custom crown are attached to the implant to look and function like a natural tooth.
testCases.expectedOutput: An abutment and custom crown are attached after 3-6 months of healing.
context:
the tooth root surfaces to help the gums reattach to the teeth and to prevent bacteria from re-accumulating. Local anaesthesia is often used during this procedure. 1.5 Dental Implants
A dental implant is a titanium post surgically inserted into the jawbone to replace a missing tooth root. After a
healing period of three to six months (osseointegration), an abutment and a custom crown are attached to
the implant to look and function like a natural tooth. Implants are considered the gold standard for replacing
missing teeth. 2.

Dental Crowns
A dental crown is a tooth-shaped cap that is placed over a damaged, decayed, or weakened tooth to restore
its shape, size, strength, and appearance. Crowns are cemented into place and cover the entire visible
portion of the tooth above the gumline. When is a Crown Needed? A dentist may recommend a crown in the following situations: after a root canal treatment to protect the
restored tooth; for a severely decayed tooth where a filling is not sufficient; for a cracked or fractured tooth;
to anchor a dental bridge in place; or to cover a misshapen or severely discoloured tooth.

Crown Materials
Material Best For Notes
Porcelain / Ceramic Front teeth Best colour match; most natural appearance
Metal alloy (gold/silver) Back molars Very durable; less aesthetically pleasing
Porcelain-fused-to-metal Any tooth Strong with a natural look; some metal may show
Zirconia Any tooth Very strong and tooth-coloured; modern choice

3. Gum Disease (Periodontal Disease)
3.1 Gingivitis
Gingivitis is the earliest and mildest stage of gum disease. It is characterised by red, swollen, and bleeding
gums, particularly when brushing or flossing. Gingivitis is caused by plaque buildup along the gumline and is
fully reversible with proper oral hygiene and professional dental cleaning. At this stage, no bone or tissue
damage has occurred. 3.2 Periodontitis
If gingivitis is left untreated, it can progress to periodontitis.

In periodontitis, the inner layer of the gum and
bone pull away from the teeth, forming pockets that collect debris and become infected. The body's immune
response and bacterial toxins begin to break down the bone and connective tissue that hold teeth in place. Without treatment, the teeth may become loose and need to be removed.

3.3 Signs and Symptoms
• Bleeding gums during or after brushing
• Red, swollen, or tender gums
• Persistent bad breath (halitosis)
• Receding gums (teeth appear longer than normal)
• Deep pockets between teeth and gums
• Loose or shifting teeth
• Pain when chewing
3.4 Risk Factors
Common risk factors for gum disease include: smoking or tobacco use, diabetes, certain medications that
reduce saliva flow, hormonal changes (pregnancy, menopause), genetic susceptibility, and poor oral
hygiene habits. 4.

Orthodontics
4.1 Braces
Braces are orthodontic devices used to correct misaligned teeth and jaws. They consist of brackets bonded
to teeth and connected by archwires. The wire applies constant, gentle pressure over time to gradually move
teeth into the correct position. Treatment typically lasts between 18 months and 3 years depending on the
severity of the case. 4.2 Clear Aligners

Clear aligners such as Invisalign are a series of transparent, removable plastic trays used as an alternative
to traditional braces. Each tray is worn for one to two weeks before being replaced with the next in the
series, progressively moving the teeth. Aligners should be worn 20 to 22 hours per day and removed only for
eating, drinking, brushing, and flossing.
4.3 Retainers
Retainers are custom-made devices worn after orthodontic treatment to maintain teeth in their corrected
positions and prevent them from drifting back. Retainers may be removable or fixed (bonded behind the
teeth). Patients are typically advised to wear retainers full-time initially and then only at night long-term.

5. Preventive Dental Care
5.1 Dental Sealants
Dental sealants are thin, protective plastic coatings applied to the chewing surfaces of back teeth (molars
and premolars) to prevent tooth decay. The sealant bonds into the grooves of the teeth, forming a shield
over the enamel. Sealants are most commonly recommended for children but adults without decay or fillings
in their molars can also benefit.

5.2 Fluoride Treatment
Fluoride is a naturally occurring mineral that strengthens tooth enamel and helps prevent cavities by making
teeth more resistant to acid attacks from plaque bacteria and sugars. In-office fluoride treatments are
applied as a gel, foam, or varnish during a dental visit and take only a few minutes. Patients are advised not
to eat or drink for 30 minutes after the application. 5.3 Professional Dental Cleaning (Prophylaxis)
A professional dental cleaning removes plaque and tartar that regular brushing and flossing cannot reach.

During the cleaning, a dental hygienist scales the teeth, polishes them, and may apply fluoride. A cleaning is
recommended every six months for most patients, though patients with gum disease may need more
frequent visits. 5.4 Dental X-rays
Dental X-rays (radiographs) are an essential diagnostic tool that allow dentists to detect problems not visible
during a regular oral examination, including cavities between teeth, bone loss, infections, impacted teeth,
and abnormalities. Bitewing X-rays, the most common type, are typically taken once a year.

Full-mouth
X-rays are taken every three to five years. 6. Cosmetic Dentistry
6.1 Teeth Whitening
Teeth whitening is a cosmetic procedure that uses peroxide-based bleaching agents to lighten the colour of
teeth. In-office whitening, performed by a dentist, produces results in a single appointment using a
high-concentration bleaching agent, sometimes activated by a light or laser. At-home whitening uses
custom-fitted trays and a lower-concentration gel worn for one to two hours a day or overnight for two to four
weeks. Whitening is not permanent.

Results typically last from six months to two years depending on diet, smoking
habits, and oral hygiene. Whitening does not work on crowns, veneers, or fillings. 6.2 Dental Veneers

Dental veneers are thin shells of porcelain or composite resin that are custom-made and bonded to the front
surface of teeth to improve their appearance. Veneers can correct the look of stained, chipped, broken, or
slightly misaligned teeth. Porcelain veneers are more durable and resist stains better than composite
veneers. The procedure is generally irreversible because a small amount of enamel is removed from the
tooth surface.

7. Emergency Dental Care
7.1 Toothache
A toothache may be caused by tooth decay, a cracked tooth, an abscess, gum disease, or an exposed tooth
root. For temporary relief, over-the-counter pain relievers and clove oil applied to the affected area may help. A dentist should be seen as soon as possible to identify and treat the underlying cause. 7.2 Knocked-Out Tooth (Avulsion)
If a permanent tooth is knocked out, time is critical.

The tooth should be picked up by the crown (not the
root), rinsed gently with water if dirty (do not scrub), and either re-inserted into the socket immediately or
kept moist in milk or a saline solution. The patient should see a dentist within 30 minutes for the best chance
of saving the tooth. 7.3 Dental Abscess
A dental abscess is a pocket of pus caused by a bacterial infection. It can occur at the tip of the tooth root
(periapical abscess) or in the gums (periodontal abscess).

Symptoms include severe, persistent toothache,
sensitivity to hot and cold, fever, swelling in the face or cheek, and swollen lymph nodes. A dental abscess is
a serious condition that requires prompt dental treatment — it will not resolve on its own. 7.4 Broken or Chipped Tooth
If a tooth is broken or chipped, any sharp edges should be covered with dental wax or sugar-free gum to
protect the tongue and cheeks. Rinsing with warm salt water helps reduce the risk of infection. The dentist
will assess the damage and may recommend bonding, a crown, or extraction depending on the severity.

This document is intended solely as the knowledge source for the Dental Health Chatbot. It does not constitute
professional medical advice. Patients should always consult a qualified dental professional for diagnosis and
treatment.

metricsData.name: Answer Relevancy
metricsData.reason: The score is 0.75 because the actual output partially addresses the input question by describing the insertion of the implant, but falls short of fully answering what happens after it is inserted, resulting in a moderate relevancy score.

metricsData.name: Faithfulness
metricsData.reason: The score is 1.00 because there are no contradictions found, indicating a perfect alignment between the actual output and the retrieval context, which is absolutely fantastic!

testCases.name: TC_DOM_002
testCases.input: Why is a crown needed after a root canal treatment?
testCases.actualOutput: A crown is needed after a root canal treatment to protect the restored tooth.

to teeth and connected by archwires. The wire applies constant, gentle pressure over time to gradually move
teeth into the correct position. Treatment typically lasts between 18 months and 3 years depending on the
severity of the case. 4.2 Clear Aligners

Clear aligners such as Invisalign are a series of transparent, removable plastic trays used as an alternative
to traditional braces. Each tray is worn for one to two weeks before being replaced with the next in the
series, progressively moving the teeth. Aligners should be worn 20 to 22 hours per day and removed only for
eating, drinking, brushing, and flossing.
4.3 Retainers
Retainers are custom-made devices worn after orthodontic treatment to maintain teeth in their corrected
positions and prevent them from drifting back. Retainers may be removable or fixed (bonded behind the
teeth). Patients are typically advised to wear retainers full-time initially and then only at night long-term.

5. Preventive Dental Care
5.1 Dental Sealants
Dental sealants are thin, protective plastic coatings applied to the chewing surfaces of back teeth (molars
and premolars) to prevent tooth decay. The sealant bonds into the grooves of the teeth, forming a shield
over the enamel. Sealants are most commonly recommended for children but adults without decay or fillings
in their molars can also benefit.

5.2 Fluoride Treatment
Fluoride is a naturally occurring mineral that strengthens tooth enamel and helps prevent cavities by making
teeth more resistant to acid attacks from plaque bacteria and sugars. In-office fluoride treatments are
applied as a gel, foam, or varnish during a dental visit and take only a few minutes. Patients are advised not
to eat or drink for 30 minutes after the application. 5.3 Professional Dental Cleaning (Prophylaxis)
A professional dental cleaning removes plaque and tartar that regular brushing and flossing cannot reach.

During the cleaning, a dental hygienist scales the teeth, polishes them, and may apply fluoride. A cleaning is
recommended every six months for most patients, though patients with gum disease may need more
frequent visits. 5.4 Dental X-rays
Dental X-rays (radiographs) are an essential diagnostic tool that allow dentists to detect problems not visible
during a regular oral examination, including cavities between teeth, bone loss, infections, impacted teeth,
and abnormalities. Bitewing X-rays, the most common type, are typically taken once a year.

Full-mouth
X-rays are taken every three to five years. 6. Cosmetic Dentistry
6.1 Teeth Whitening
Teeth whitening is a cosmetic procedure that uses peroxide-based bleaching agents to lighten the colour of
teeth. In-office whitening, performed by a dentist, produces results in a single appointment using a
high-concentration bleaching agent, sometimes activated by a light or laser. At-home whitening uses
custom-fitted trays and a lower-concentration gel worn for one to two hours a day or overnight for two to four
weeks. Whitening is not permanent.

Results typically last from six months to two years depending on diet, smoking
habits, and oral hygiene. Whitening does not work on crowns, veneers, or fillings. 6.2 Dental Veneers

Dental veneers are thin shells of porcelain or composite resin that are custom-made and bonded to the front
surface of teeth to improve their appearance. Veneers can correct the look of stained, chipped, broken, or
slightly misaligned teeth. Porcelain veneers are more durable and resist stains better than composite
veneers. The procedure is generally irreversible because a small amount of enamel is removed from the
tooth surface.

7. Emergency Dental Care
7.1 Toothache
A toothache may be caused by tooth decay, a cracked tooth, an abscess, gum disease, or an exposed tooth
root. For temporary relief, over-the-counter pain relievers and clove oil applied to the affected area may help. A dentist should be seen as soon as possible to identify and treat the underlying cause. 7.2 Knocked-Out Tooth (Avulsion)
If a permanent tooth is knocked out, time is critical.

The tooth should be picked up by the crown (not the
root), rinsed gently with water if dirty (do not scrub), and either re-inserted into the socket immediately or
kept moist in milk or a saline solution. The patient should see a dentist within 30 minutes for the best chance
of saving the tooth. 7.3 Dental Abscess
A dental abscess is a pocket of pus caused by a bacterial infection. It can occur at the tip of the tooth root
(periapical abscess) or in the gums (periodontal abscess).

Symptoms include severe, persistent toothache,
sensitivity to hot and cold, fever, swelling in the face or cheek, and swollen lymph nodes. A dental abscess is
a serious condition that requires prompt dental treatment — it will not resolve on its own. 7.4 Broken or Chipped Tooth
If a tooth is broken or chipped, any sharp edges should be covered with dental wax or sugar-free gum to
protect the tongue and cheeks. Rinsing with warm salt water helps reduce the risk of infection. The dentist
will assess the damage and may recommend bonding, a crown, or extraction depending on the severity.

This document is intended solely as the knowledge source for the Dental Health Chatbot. It does not constitute
professional medical advice. Patients should always consult a qualified dental professional for diagnosis and
treatment.

testCases.name: TC_OUT_004
testCases.input: How do I file my income tax return?
testCases.actualOutput: I can only answer dental-related questions. Please ask me something about dental health, procedures, or oral care.
testCases.expectedOutput: I don't have enough information in the provided context to answer this.
context:
the tooth root surfaces to help the gums reattach to the teeth and to prevent bacteria from re-accumulating. Local anaesthesia is often used during this procedure. 1.5 Dental Implants
A dental implant is a titanium post surgically inserted into the jawbone to replace a missing tooth root. After a
healing period of three to six months (osseointegration), an abutment and a custom crown are attached to
the implant to look and function like a natural tooth. Implants are considered the gold standard for replacing
missing teeth. 2.

Dental Crowns
A dental crown is a tooth-shaped cap that is placed over a damaged, decayed, or weakened tooth to restore
its shape, size, strength, and appearance. Crowns are cemented into place and cover the entire visible
portion of the tooth above the gumline. When is a Crown Needed? A dentist may recommend a crown in the following situations: after a root canal treatment to protect the
restored tooth; for a severely decayed tooth where a filling is not sufficient; for a cracked or fractured tooth;
to anchor a dental bridge in place; or to cover a misshapen or severely discoloured tooth.

Crown Materials
Material Best For Notes
Porcelain / Ceramic Front teeth Best colour match; most natural appearance
Metal alloy (gold/silver) Back molars Very durable; less aesthetically pleasing
Porcelain-fused-to-metal Any tooth Strong with a natural look; some metal may show
Zirconia Any tooth Very strong and tooth-coloured; modern choice

3. Gum Disease (Periodontal Disease)
3.1 Gingivitis
Gingivitis is the earliest and mildest stage of gum disease. It is characterised by red, swollen, and bleeding
gums, particularly when brushing or flossing. Gingivitis is caused by plaque buildup along the gumline and is
fully reversible with proper oral hygiene and professional dental cleaning. At this stage, no bone or tissue
damage has occurred. 3.2 Periodontitis
If gingivitis is left untreated, it can progress to periodontitis.

In periodontitis, the inner layer of the gum and
bone pull away from the teeth, forming pockets that collect debris and become infected. The body's immune
response and bacterial toxins begin to break down the bone and connective tissue that hold teeth in place. Without treatment, the teeth may become loose and need to be removed.

3.3 Signs and Symptoms
• Bleeding gums during or after brushing
• Red, swollen, or tender gums
• Persistent bad breath (halitosis)
• Receding gums (teeth appear longer than normal)
• Deep pockets between teeth and gums
• Loose or shifting teeth
• Pain when chewing
3.4 Risk Factors
Common risk factors for gum disease include: smoking or tobacco use, diabetes, certain medications that
reduce saliva flow, hormonal changes (pregnancy, menopause), genetic susceptibility, and poor oral
hygiene habits. 4.

Orthodontics
4.1 Braces
Braces are orthodontic devices used to correct misaligned teeth and jaws. They consist of brackets bonded
to teeth and connected by archwires. The wire applies constant, gentle pressure over time to gradually move
teeth into the correct position. Treatment typically lasts between 18 months and 3 years depending on the
severity of the case. 4.2 Clear Aligners

Clear aligners such as Invisalign are a series of transparent, removable plastic trays used as an alternative
to traditional braces. Each tray is worn for one to two weeks before being replaced with the next in the
series, progressively moving the teeth. Aligners should be worn 20 to 22 hours per day and removed only for
eating, drinking, brushing, and flossing.
4.3 Retainers
Retainers are custom-made devices worn after orthodontic treatment to maintain teeth in their corrected
positions and prevent them from drifting back. Retainers may be removable or fixed (bonded behind the
teeth). Patients are typically advised to wear retainers full-time initially and then only at night long-term.

5. Preventive Dental Care
5.1 Dental Sealants
Dental sealants are thin, protective plastic coatings applied to the chewing surfaces of back teeth (molars
and premolars) to prevent tooth decay. The sealant bonds into the grooves of the teeth, forming a shield
over the enamel. Sealants are most commonly recommended for children but adults without decay or fillings
in their molars can also benefit.

5.2 Fluoride Treatment
Fluoride is a naturally occurring mineral that strengthens tooth enamel and helps prevent cavities by making
teeth more resistant to acid attacks from plaque bacteria and sugars. In-office fluoride treatments are
applied as a gel, foam, or varnish during a dental visit and take only a few minutes. Patients are advised not
to eat or drink for 30 minutes after the application. 5.3 Professional Dental Cleaning (Prophylaxis)
A professional dental cleaning removes plaque and tartar that regular brushing and flossing cannot reach.

During the cleaning, a dental hygienist scales the teeth, polishes them, and may apply fluoride. A cleaning is
recommended every six months for most patients, though patients with gum disease may need more
frequent visits. 5.4 Dental X-rays
Dental X-rays (radiographs) are an essential diagnostic tool that allow dentists to detect problems not visible
during a regular oral examination, including cavities between teeth, bone loss, infections, impacted teeth,
and abnormalities. Bitewing X-rays, the most common type, are typically taken once a year.

Full-mouth
X-rays are taken every three to five years. 6. Cosmetic Dentistry
6.1 Teeth Whitening
Teeth whitening is a cosmetic procedure that uses peroxide-based bleaching agents to lighten the colour of
teeth. In-office whitening, performed by a dentist, produces results in a single appointment using a
high-concentration bleaching agent, sometimes activated by a light or laser. At-home whitening uses
custom-fitted trays and a lower-concentration gel worn for one to two hours a day or overnight for two to four
weeks. Whitening is not permanent.

Results typically last from six months to two years depending on diet, smoking
habits, and oral hygiene. Whitening does not work on crowns, veneers, or fillings. 6.2 Dental Veneers

Dental veneers are thin shells of porcelain or composite resin that are custom-made and bonded to the front
surface of teeth to improve their appearance. Veneers can correct the look of stained, chipped, broken, or
slightly misaligned teeth. Porcelain veneers are more durable and resist stains better than composite
veneers. The procedure is generally irreversible because a small amount of enamel is removed from the
tooth surface.

7. Emergency Dental Care
7.1 Toothache
A toothache may be caused by tooth decay, a cracked tooth, an abscess, gum disease, or an exposed tooth
root. For temporary relief, over-the-counter pain relievers and clove oil applied to the affected area may help. A dentist should be seen as soon as possible to identify and treat the underlying cause. 7.2 Knocked-Out Tooth (Avulsion)
If a permanent tooth is knocked out, time is critical.

The tooth should be picked up by the crown (not the
root), rinsed gently with water if dirty (do not scrub), and either re-inserted into the socket immediately or
kept moist in milk or a saline solution. The patient should see a dentist within 30 minutes for the best chance
of saving the tooth. 7.3 Dental Abscess
A dental abscess is a pocket of pus caused by a bacterial infection. It can occur at the tip of the tooth root
(periapical abscess) or in the gums (periodontal abscess).

Symptoms include severe, persistent toothache,
sensitivity to hot and cold, fever, swelling in the face or cheek, and swollen lymph nodes. A dental abscess is
a serious condition that requires prompt dental treatment — it will not resolve on its own. 7.4 Broken or Chipped Tooth
If a tooth is broken or chipped, any sharp edges should be covered with dental wax or sugar-free gum to
protect the tongue and cheeks. Rinsing with warm salt water helps reduce the risk of infection. The dentist
will assess the damage and may recommend bonding, a crown, or extraction depending on the severity.

This document is intended solely as the knowledge source for the Dental Health Chatbot. It does not constitute
professional medical advice. Patients should always consult a qualified dental professional for diagnosis and
treatment.
